proposals.contribution: This work will directly contribute to the operationalization of soil security by identifying concrete land management and soil types that preserve microbial diversity, a key component of soil condition. It will provide a spatially explicit, evidence-based framework for prioritizing land use planning and soil management interventions to mitigate biological homogenization. The findings will advance the concept of 'soil security buffering capacity' as a measurable ecosystem service.
proposals.novelty: While biotic homogenization is recognized, this study uniquely tests the *buffering* capacity of management and soil type as moderating variables, moving beyond documentation of the pattern to identification of solutions. It innovatively integrates high-throughput molecular ecology with DSM principles to scale microbial community dynamics for global soil security assessment.

proposals.title: Ground-truthing the digital soil genus: A continental-scale validation of high-resolution DSMART maps across Australian bioregions
proposals.motivation: The development of high-resolution digital soil maps, such as those produced by the DSMART (Disaggregation and Harmonisation of Soil Map Units Through Resampled Classification Trees) algorithm, promises a revolution in soil security and land management by providing genus-level predictions at continental scales. However, their operational utility for critical decisions in agriculture, environmental conservation, and climate adaptation depends entirely on their accuracy, which remains largely untested with independent, spatially representative field data. This validation gap undermines confidence in these foundational digital public goods for global soil security.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the predicted soil genus classes from continental-scale DSMART maps will demonstrate statistically significant agreement (>70% accuracy at the genus level) with independent field observations, but that accuracy will vary systematically with landscape complexity, legacy soil map quality, and the density of the original point data used for disaggregation.
proposals.method: We will design a stratified random sampling campaign across 50 key Australian bioregions, targeting areas of high and low prediction uncertainty from the DSMART model. At each of 500 new, independent sites, soil will be described and classified to family and genus level according to the Australian Soil Classification. These observations will form a validation dataset completely independent of the model's training data. Accuracy will be quantified using error matrices, Cohen's Kappa, and spatial analysis of error clusters. We will then use Random Forest regression to model validation accuracy as a function of environmental covariates (terrain, climate, legacy map scale) to diagnose systematic error sources.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first rigorous, continental-scale 'fitness-for-purpose' assessment of high-resolution digital soil genus maps for Australia. It will directly inform the Global Soil Partnership's efforts towards digital soil mapping capacity building by establishing benchmark validation protocols. The results will critically advance the field of pedometrics by quantifying the real-world limits of disaggregation techniques and providing a transparent error model for end-users in policy and management.
proposals.novelty: While DSMART and similar algorithms are widely used, their validation typically relies on internal cross-validation or limited, non-representative field checks. This proposal is novel in its commitment to a major, independent field campaign designed explicitly for statistical validation across the full range of mapped conditions, moving beyond computational performance to assess actual ground-truth accuracy at the management-relevant genus scale.

proposals.title: Evaluating the Efficacy of Genus-Level Digital Soil Maps for Targeting Precision Soil Security Interventions
proposals.motivation: Achieving soil security requires management strategies that are both globally scalable and locally precise. Pre-2026 digital soil mapping (DSM) has advanced to create high-resolution maps of soil properties, but translating these maps into actionable, field-scale interventions remains a significant challenge. This gap limits our ability to implement the 'DSMA' (Digital Soil Mapping and Assessment) paradigm effectively for tangible improvements in soil condition and global food security.
proposals.hypothesis: That management interventions (e.g., specific organic amendments or tillage practices) targeted using genus-level soil distribution maps will yield significantly greater improvements in key soil security indicators (e.g., aggregate stability, carbon content) compared to conventional, non-targeted management.
proposals.method: We will design a multi-continent field trial across sites representing distinct soil genera. For each site, genus maps derived from legacy soil data and covariates (e.g., DEM, Landsat) will guide the placement of paired treatment and control plots. Interventions will be selected based on the diagnosed constraints of each soil genus (e.g., compost addition for Arenosols). Soil response will be quantified using pre-2026 accessible digital methods, including the mobile phone-based aggregate stability measurement protocol developed by the team. Statistical analysis will employ linear mixed-effects models to isolate the effect of map-targeted intervention.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first empirical, field-validated framework for operationalizing digital soil genus maps into precision land management. It will advance the scientific basis for 'Peak Soil' mitigation by demonstrating a pathway to enhance soil condition efficiently. The project will also build global capacity by providing a tested protocol for using ubiquitous digital tools (phones, open-source maps) to implement and monitor soil security actions.
proposals.novelty: While genus maps exist, their use as direct blueprints for prescribing and testing specific physical soil interventions is novel. The research uniquely bridges the digital (maps) and physical (field management) realms of soil security, moving beyond correlation to causation in DSM application.

proposals.title: Mapping the Soil Microbiome Niche: A Species Distribution Modeling Approach to Predict Bacterial Genus Suitability for Soil Security
proposals.motivation: Soil security requires understanding the functional condition of the soil microbiome, a key determinant of ecosystem services and agricultural productivity. Pre-2026, macroecology has successfully used Species Distribution Models (SDMs) to predict plant and animal ranges from environmental covariates, but their application to soil bacteria at a global scale remains nascent. This gap limits our ability to forecast microbial responses to land management and climate change, hindering proactive soil management strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the environmental niches of key bacterial genera, defined by soil properties (e.g., pH, texture, organic carbon) and climate variables, can be robustly modeled using SDMs, and that these models will reveal genera-specific suitability patterns critical for soil function and security.
proposals.method: We will apply the Maximum Entropy (MaxEnt) algorithm, a leading presence-background SDM, to global soil microbiome sequence datasets (e.g., from Earth Microbiome Project) linked with spatial environmental layers from SoilGrids and WorldClim. For target bacterial genera, we will use genus presence/relative abundance data as the response variable against a suite of pedological, climatic, and land-use predictors. Model performance will be evaluated via spatial cross-validation and AUC metrics. The final output will be global, high-resolution (250m) predictive maps of niche suitability for each target genus.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first globally consistent, pedologically-informed suitability maps for soil bacterial genera, directly contributing to the digital soil assessment framework. It will establish a novel methodological bridge between macroecology and soil microbiology, providing a predictive tool to assess microbial community condition and its role in soil functions under global change scenarios.
proposals.novelty: While SDMs are standard in macroecology, their systematic application to model the distribution of soil bacterial genera based on *in-situ* soil properties (rather than just climate) at a global scale is a novel synthesis. This directly operationalizes the 'Peak Soil' and soil security concepts by providing a quantifiable, spatial method to assess a core component of soil biological capital.

proposals.title: Mapping the Global Distributions of Key Soil Fungal and Archaeal Genera: A Digital Soil Mapping Approach for Soil Biological Security
proposals.motivation: Soil security requires understanding not just physical and chemical properties, but also the biological condition underpinning soil functions like carbon sequestration and nutrient cycling. Pre-2026, global digital soil mapping (DSM) efforts have focused predominantly on abiotic factors, leaving a critical gap in predictive, spatially explicit knowledge of soil microbial biogeography at management-relevant scales. This gap hinders our capacity to assess the vulnerability of soil biological communities to global change and manage for soil health.
proposals.hypothesis: That the distribution of dominant soil fungal and archaeal genera can be robustly predicted at continental to global scales using a DSM framework that integrates environmental covariates (climate, terrain, soil properties) with legacy microbial community data. We hypothesize that fungal distributions will be more strongly linked to organic matter and vegetation, while archaeal distributions will correlate more with soil pH and mineralogy.
proposals.method: We will adapt a proven DSM machine learning pipeline, such as Random Forest or Gradient Boosting, previously used for soil properties. The pipeline will be applied to a global compilation of soil amplicon sequencing data (e.g., from Earth Microbiome Project, NCBI) to model the relative abundance or presence/absence of target genera. Key covariates will include WorldClim climate layers, SoilGrids-derived physicochemical properties, remote sensing indices (MODIS), and digital terrain attributes. Model performance will be evaluated via spatial cross-validation to ensure generalizability.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first set of spatially continuous, predictive maps for critical soil fungal and archaeal taxa, directly contributing to the 'biological' dimension of digital soil assessment. It will establish a methodological framework for integrating microbial ecology into soil security science, providing a new tool for monitoring soil biological condition and its role in ecosystem services under global change scenarios.
proposals.novelty: While DSM is mature for abiotic factors, its application to predict specific microbial taxa at broad scales is novel. The dual focus on both fungi and archaea within the same analytical framework will allow for a comparative analysis of the environmental drivers controlling these key biological components of soil security.

proposals.title: Beyond Spectral Activity: A Digital Soil Mapping Framework for Soil Security Using Indirect Proxies and Ensemble Models
proposals.motivation: Digital soil mapping (DSM) predominantly relies on spectrally active properties, creating a critical gap for key soil security indicators like aggregate stability, soil strength, and specific mineralogy that lack direct spectral signatures. This limitation hinders the global, high-resolution assessment of soil condition and its role in food and environmental security. Addressing this gap is essential for moving beyond 'peak soil' concepts to actionable, management-focused soil intelligence.
proposals.hypothesis: Soil properties with weak or no direct spectral response can be predicted with acceptable accuracy by leveraging their functional relationships with spectrally active 'surrogate' properties within a spatially explicit, ensemble modelling framework.
proposals.method: We will develop a hybrid modelling architecture combining geostatistics, machine learning, and pedotransfer rules. Core spectrally active properties (e.g., clay, iron oxides, organic carbon) will be mapped from legacy and new mobile phone-based vis-NIR data. These will serve as inputs into: 1) a Random Forest model trained on a global soil spectral library with measured 'target' properties, and 2) a rule-based pedotransfer function layer encoding known physico-chemical relationships (e.g., clay mineralogy and organic matter influencing aggregate stability). A Bayesian model averaging ensemble will integrate these predictions with spatial covariates (terrain, climate) to produce final maps with uncertainty estimates.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a methodological blueprint for expanding the scope of DSM to critical, spectrally inactive soil attributes, directly enhancing global soil security assessment capacity. It will yield a scalable, digital framework that integrates scientific pedological knowledge with data-driven models, moving soil condition monitoring beyond direct spectral proxies.
proposals.novelty: The approach explicitly relaxes the foundational DSM assumption of direct spectral activity, systematically formalizing the use of indirect proxies within an ensemble digital mapping workflow, which is a significant departure from conventional correlative spectral modelling.

proposals.title: Calibrating Soil Security: Adapting Pharmaceutical Chemometrics for Robust Spectral Analysis of Complex Soil Mixtures
proposals.motivation: Soil security requires rapid, accurate, and globally scalable digital soil assessments. Current chemometric models for soil spectral libraries (e.g., using Vis-NIR-MIR) often struggle with the extreme heterogeneity and complex matrix effects inherent in global soil systems, leading to calibration transfer problems and reduced predictive capacity for new sites. Advanced chemometric techniques developed for pharmaceutical quality control, where precision for complex mixtures is paramount, offer a potential solution to this critical bottleneck in digital soil mapping.
proposals.hypothesis: That chemometric models specifically designed for pharmaceutical analysis—such as Orthogonal Partial Least Squares (O-PLS) and Independent Component Analysis (ICA)—can be adapted to significantly improve the robustness and transferability of spectral calibrations for predicting key soil condition attributes (e.g., organic carbon, clay content) across diverse global landscapes.
proposals.method: We will adapt and test two pharmaceutical chemometric frameworks. First, O-PLS will be used to separate structured soil spectral variance from orthogonal noise arising from variable moisture, particle size, and mineralogy. Second, ICA will be employed to deconvolute spectral signatures from overlapping soil constituents, treating them as independent sources. These methods will be applied to a pre-existing global Vis-NIR spectral library, benchmarking their predictive performance and transferability against standard PLSR and machine learning models for core soil properties.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel methodological bridge between pharmaceutical and pedometric sciences, delivering more robust calibration protocols for global digital soil mapping. It will directly enhance the capacity for reliable, large-scale soil condition monitoring, a cornerstone for informed soil management and security. The project will yield open-source code and validation datasets to support the wider soil security community.
proposals.novelty: While chemometrics is used in soil science, the direct adaptation of sophisticated pharmaceutical models like O-PLS and ICA, which are explicitly designed for complex mixture analysis and signal deconvolution, to address soil spectral complexity and calibration transfer is a novel and non-obvious cross-disciplinary innovation.

proposals.title: Inverting the Soil Paradigm: A Generative Model to Derive Theoretical Spectral Signatures for Optimal Soil Condition and Capacity
proposals.motivation: Digital soil assessment, particularly via proximal and remote sensing, relies on correlating spectral data to soil properties and functions. This forward approach is limited by the empirical spectra of existing, often degraded, soils. To guide soil management towards achieving the goals of soil security—such as optimal condition for defined capacities—we need a theoretical target. This proposal addresses the inverse problem: defining the spectral signature a soil *should* have to fulfill its optimal functional role, providing a quantifiable benchmark for restoration and management.
proposals.hypothesis: A soil's optimal functional state, defined by quantifiable capacity and condition parameters (e.g., water holding capacity, aggregate stability, carbon content), corresponds to a theoretically derivable and spectrally unique signature across the visible-near-infrared-shortwave infrared (VNIR-SWIR) range. This signature can be modeled by inverting established pedotransfer and radiative transfer relationships.
proposals.method: We will develop a generative model using a physics-informed neural network (PINN) architecture. First, we will define a multi-dimensional 'optimal soil state vector' for key functions (e.g., productivity, filtration) based on pre-2026 soil security frameworks. Core pedotransfer functions and Mie scattering/radiative transfer models (e.g., Hapke model) will be embedded as physical constraints within the network. The PINN will be trained to invert these relationships, learning the mapping from the optimal state vector to a corresponding synthetic reflectance spectrum (350-2500 nm). Validation will involve comparing model outputs against spectra from high-quality benchmark soils and using the derived spectra to predict functional parameters in independent datasets via standard chemometric models (e.g., PLSR).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative, spectrally-defined target for 'good' soil, moving beyond relative assessments. It will contribute a novel generative tool to the digital soil assessment toolkit, enabling the setting of spectral benchmarks for soil security. The theoretical spectra will offer a new reference for calibrating remote sensing platforms and designing sensor networks aimed at monitoring soil condition against an optimal standard.
proposals.novelty: While vast literature uses spectra to predict soil properties, inverting the process to generate a target spectrum from a defined functional optimum is a fundamentally novel direction. It integrates the conceptual frameworks of soil security and capacity with the technical rigor of radiative transfer modeling and modern generative AI in a way not previously attempted.

proposals.title: A Digital Soil Security Framework: Quantifying the Impact of Structural Heterogeneity and Preferential Flow on Contaminant Fate and Transport
proposals.motivation: The assumption of a homogeneous soil column is a critical simplification in many models of solute and contaminant transport, yet real-world soil security is threatened by the movement of pollutants, nutrients, and agrochemicals through structured, heterogeneous profiles. Pre-2026, the digital soil assessment revolution provides new tools to characterize this complexity, but its integration into predictive, management-ready models for soil security remains underdeveloped. This gap limits our capacity to accurately assess risks to soil condition, water quality, and global food systems.
proposals.hypothesis: Explicitly modelling soil profile heterogeneity and preferential flow paths derived from digital soil morphometrics will significantly improve the prediction of reactant transport dynamics compared to homogeneous column models, thereby providing a more secure basis for land management decisions.
proposals.method: The research will employ a coupled digital and physical modelling approach. First, soil structural data (aggregate size distribution, porosity, visible macropores) will be captured non-destructively using smartphone-based photogrammetry and image analysis, building on McBratney's prior work. This digital soil morphometric data will inform the parameterization of a 2D/3D dual-permeability model (e.g., using HYDRUS-2D/3D with the mobile-immobile water or macroporosity modules). The model will be validated against laboratory and field tracer experiments on soils with contrasting structural conditions. Sensitivity analysis will identify the key digital metrics most critical for predicting preferential flow.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a practical, digitally-enabled framework for moving beyond the homogeneous column paradigm in soil security science. It will deliver a validated methodology linking in-situ digital assessment to improved transport predictions, directly enhancing the capacity for managing soil pollution and nutrient use efficiency. The outcomes will strengthen the scientific role of digital soil assessment in addressing global soil security challenges.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct integration of low-cost, field-deployable digital soil morphometric techniques (e.g., mobile phone-based aggregate stability and structure analysis) into mechanistic transport models, creating a feedback loop between rapid condition assessment and predictive security modelling that was not standard practice pre-2026.

proposals.title: A Digital Soil Mapping Approach to Quantify and Predict Reactive Mineral Surface Area Dynamics for Soil Security
proposals.motivation: Soil security requires understanding the fundamental chemical and physical processes governing nutrient cycling, carbon sequestration, and contaminant binding. Pre-2026 models often treat soil mineral reactivity as static, ignoring how management, erosion, and aggregation dynamically alter the reactive surface area of clays and oxides. This gap limits our ability to predict soil function and resilience under global change, a core concern of the soil security framework.
proposals.hypothesis: The reactive surface area of key soil minerals can be predicted at landscape scales by integrating proximal sensor data (e.g., vis-NIR, XRF) with pedotransfer functions and temporal land management data, and that incorporating this dynamic property will significantly improve digital soil models for cation exchange capacity and phosphorus sorption.
proposals.method: We will develop a hybrid DSMa (Digital Soil Mapping and Assessment) workflow. First, we will establish a reference dataset of mineral-specific surface area (via BET-N2 and EGME) for diverse soil samples. Using pre-2026 machine learning architectures (specifically, Cubist regression trees and Random Forests), we will model surface area as a function of spectroscopic data and conventional soil properties. This model will be applied to spatially exhaustive sensor data. Temporal change will be incorporated by linking surface area predictions to land management history and aggregate stability indices, the latter potentially derived from the researcher's prior work on mobile phone-based aggregate stability measurement.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first spatially explicit, time-integrated maps of a key soil intrinsic property—reactive surface area—directly supporting the 'condition' and 'capability' dimensions of soil security. It will provide a new, quantifiable state variable for global soil health assessments and improve the mechanistic basis of digital soil function models.
proposals.novelty: While DSMa predicts static properties like clay content, dynamically modelling the reactive *interface* of those minerals over time is novel. It moves beyond composition to functional geometry, directly linking the researcher's themes of digital capacity, soil condition, and global security.

proposals.title: Re-framing soil security: A global assessment of calcium limitation on soil organic carbon stabilization and aggregate dynamics
proposals.motivation: Soil security frameworks have long focused on nitrogen and phosphorus as primary limiting nutrients for productivity and carbon sequestration. However, in calcareous, highly weathered, or acidic soils amended with high carbon inputs, calcium availability can become the critical constraint for forming stable organo-mineral associations and aggregates. This overlooked limitation has direct implications for global carbon models and management strategies aimed at soil-based climate mitigation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that in key global soil regions, calcium availability, not carbon or nitrogen, is the primary limiting factor for the formation of calcium-mediated soil organic matter (SOM) complexes and water-stable aggregates, thereby controlling medium-term soil carbon stabilization capacity.
proposals.method: We will employ a digital soil mapping (DSM) approach, integrating global spatial databases (SoilGrids, ISRIC) of soil calcium forms, pH, and texture. A conditional random forest algorithm will model regions of potential calcium limitation. For validation, we will develop a standardized, low-cost field assay using mobile phone image analysis of aggregate stability (building on prior work) paired with calcium saturation tests on soil samples from predicted hotspots. Controlled incubation experiments will quantify the carbon sequestration response to calcium addition versus nitrogen/phosphorus.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first global spatial framework identifying soils where carbon management is constrained by calcium, directly informing the 'condition' dimension of soil security. It will advance the concept of 'Peak Soil' by defining a new biogeochemical limit. The method contributes to digital soil assessment capacity by providing a new diagnostic tool for extension services.
proposals.novelty: It inverts the classic nutrient limitation paradigm in soil carbon science by positioning calcium as a primary limiting element, moving beyond agronomic yield to focus on carbon stabilization mechanisms. The integration of predictive DSM with accessible phone-based phenotyping creates a novel, scalable scientific workflow for testing this hypothesis.

proposals.title: A Digital Soil Security Framework: Integrating Multi-Pathway Microbial Carbonate Dynamics for Global Soil Condition Management
proposals.motivation: Soil security is a global challenge, with soil degradation threatening food production and ecosystem services. Microbial Induced Carbonate Precipitation (MICP) is a promising bio-mediated soil improvement technique, but its current conceptualization is overly narrow, focusing primarily on ureolysis. This limits its predictive power and practical application for managing soil condition at scale. A more comprehensive digital framework is needed to account for the full suite of microbial metabolic pathways (e.g., denitrification, sulfate reduction, photosynthesis) that influence carbonate dynamics and soil structure.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating multiple, interacting microbial metabolic pathways into a digital reaction network model will significantly improve the prediction and management of soil aggregate stability and carbonate cementation under diverse environmental conditions. This expanded model will provide a more robust scientific basis for using microbial processes as a lever for soil security.
proposals.method: We will develop a process-based, agent-informed digital model using a hybrid architecture. The core will be a biogeochemical reaction network (using stoichiometric matrix methods, pre-2026) that incorporates kinetic rates for urea hydrolysis, denitrification, sulfate reduction, and aerobic respiration as they influence pH and carbonate saturation. This will be coupled with a simplified agent-based layer to simulate microbial community dynamics. The model will be parameterized and validated using existing global soil biogeochemical datasets and new, low-cost measurements of aggregate stability derived from the mobile phone method previously developed by the team.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel digital soil management tool that moves beyond single-pathway MICP, providing a systems-level understanding of microbially mediated soil strengthening. It will establish a scientific framework for 'next-generation' bio-cementation strategies tailored to specific soil conditions and security goals. The project will also demonstrate the role of digital technology and capacity building in making advanced soil condition assessment globally accessible.
proposals.novelty: While MICP is well-studied, no pre-2026 digital management framework exists that integrates the competitive and synergistic effects of multiple microbial metabolic pathways on carbonate precipitation for soil security. The novelty lies in merging detailed biogeochemical network modeling with the practical, global-scale application ethos of digital soil assessment.

proposals.title: A coupled model of soil inorganic and organic carbon dynamics for assessing soil security under global change
proposals.motivation: Soil security requires managing the entire soil carbon pool, yet inorganic carbon (SIC) and organic carbon (SOC) are typically modelled in isolation. This is a critical gap, as pre-2026 research highlights strong geochemical and biological interactions between these pools (e.g., via acidification, carbonate dissolution, and priming effects). A unified model is essential for predicting the net soil carbon response to climate change, land management, and carbon sequestration strategies, which is fundamental for global soil security.
proposals.hypothesis: Explicitly coupling the dynamics of soil inorganic and organic carbon will significantly improve the prediction of total soil carbon stocks and their vulnerability to environmental change, compared to models treating each pool independently. We hypothesize that the coupling will reveal non-linear feedbacks, particularly in calcareous and semi-arid soils, that alter projected carbon trajectories.
proposals.method: We will develop a process-based model by integrating a mechanistic SIC module (handling dissolution-precipitation, CO2 exchange) with an established SOC turnover model (e.g., a modified RothC or CENTURY architecture). The coupling will be implemented through shared state variables: soil pH, partial pressure of CO2, and dissolved inorganic carbon. Numerical integration will use a daily time-step ODE solver. The model will be parameterized and validated using global datasets (e.g., ISRIC, ICOS) and tested across a DSMa (Digital Soil Mapping and Assessment) framework to produce spatially explicit vulnerability maps.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first open-source, process-based model framework that dynamically couples SIC and SOC. It will deliver new insights into the role of SIC as a buffer or source in the global carbon cycle, directly informing soil management for security. The resulting digital tool will enhance capacity for assessing soil condition and its role in climate mitigation.
proposals.novelty: While SOC models are mature and SIC models exist, their integration into a single, interacting system for global soil security assessment is novel. This moves beyond the prevailing paradigm of treating these major carbon pools as separate, non-communicating entities.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Sensitivity of Soil Security Outcomes to Initial Geochemical Conditions: A Global Digital Soil Mapping Approach
proposals.motivation: Soil security, a pre-2026 framework championed by McBratney, links soil condition to global challenges like food and water security. However, predictive models for soil management often treat initial geochemistry as a fixed input, neglecting its inherent spatial variability and uncertainty. This gap limits our ability to forecast the robustness of soil security interventions under different starting conditions, a critical knowledge deficit for sustainable land management.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the long-term trajectories of key soil security indicators (e.g., carbon sequestration potential, nutrient cycling resilience) are non-linearly sensitive to variations in initial soil geochemistry, and that this sensitivity varies systematically across major global soil groups and land uses.
proposals.method: We will leverage global digital soil mapping (DSM) products (e.g., SoilGrids) to define probabilistic distributions of initial geochemical properties (pH, CEC, SOC, major cations). Using a structured variance-based global sensitivity analysis (Sobol' indices), we will propagate these distributions through a process-based soil model (e.g., RothC or a simplified nutrient cycling model). The analysis will be conducted across a stratified global sample of pixels representing key DSMAs (Digital Soil Mapping Areas), with model outputs focused on 20-year projections of soil functions.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first global-scale sensitivity maps identifying where soil security forecasts are most vulnerable to uncertainty in initial conditions. It will provide a formal methodology for integrating uncertainty from legacy data into soil security planning. The results will directly inform the 'condition' and 'capability' dimensions of soil security by highlighting critical data needs for robust management.
proposals.novelty: While sensitivity analysis is common in modeling, its systematic application to the 'initial condition problem' in a global soil security context is novel. It uniquely combines the DSM paradigm with variance-based sensitivity methods to address a core, yet under-explored, uncertainty in predicting soil's role in global sustainability.

proposals.title: Agent-Based Modeling of Soil Microbial Dynamics: A Digital Framework for Quantifying Soil Condition and Security
proposals.motivation: Soil security, a core concept in McBratney's work, depends critically on the biological condition of soil, with microbial communities driving key functions like carbon cycling and aggregate stability. However, pre-2026 soil management models often treat microbes as homogeneous pools, failing to capture the individual-scale interactions that govern system-level resilience. This gap limits our ability to predict soil's response to global stressors, a central concern of the 'Peak Soil' discourse.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that an agent-based model (ABM) simulating individual microbial cells and their interactions with soil mineral surfaces and organic matter will reveal emergent properties of soil aggregate stability and carbon sequestration that are not predictable from bulk-scale models.
proposals.method: We will develop a spatially explicit ABM using the NetLogo platform, informed by empirical data on microbial physiology and soil structure. Agents (microbial cells) will be programmed with traits for growth, motility, and extracellular polymeric substance (EPS) production, interacting within a 3D digital soil matrix representing pore spaces and mineral particles. The model will integrate algorithms for nutrient diffusion, cell-cell communication, and attachment/detachment dynamics to simulate the formation and disintegration of micro-aggregates under varying moisture and carbon input scenarios.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel digital tool for quantifying the biological mechanisms underpinning soil condition, directly supporting the 'dsma' (digital soil morphometric assessment) paradigm. It will provide a mechanistic, process-based link between microbial ecology and soil physical security, offering a new capacity to model soil's role in global carbon and water cycles under changing management.
proposals.novelty: While ABMs are established in ecology, their application to simulate individual microbial cells within a physically explicit soil architecture to predict macro-scale soil security indicators (like aggregate stability) is novel. This moves beyond correlative 'black-box' models to a mechanistic, individual-based digital representation of soil biology.

proposals.title: A Digital Soil Mapping Framework for Landscape-Scale Prediction of Soil Inorganic Carbon Stocks: Integrating Mechanistic Pedogenesis and Machine Learning
proposals.motivation: Soil inorganic carbon (SIC) is a critical but poorly quantified component of the global carbon cycle, especially in arid and semi-arid regions. Its stock at landscape and watershed scales is a key indicator for soil security, influencing soil condition, agricultural productivity, and climate feedbacks. Current regional SIC estimates are often based on coarse spatial data or simple statistical models, lacking the mechanistic understanding needed for robust prediction under changing management and climate.
proposals.hypothesis: That a hybrid model integrating a mechanistic pedogenic model for SIC formation (e.g., based on carbonate solubility and leaching regimes) with a machine learning-based digital soil mapping (DSM) framework can significantly improve the accuracy and spatial resolution of regional SIC stock predictions compared to purely data-driven or purely process-based approaches.
proposals.method: We will employ a DSM framework using Quantile Regression Forest or Cubist algorithms, trained on legacy and newly collected SIC profile data. Key environmental covariates (pre-2026) will include high-resolution climate data (aridity index, temperature), digital elevation models (topographic wetness index), parent material maps, and satellite-derived vegetation/land use indices. The mechanistic component will be embedded by using outputs from a simplified, parameter-sparse geochemical model (simulating carbonate precipitation/dissolution) as an additional, informative covariate in the machine learning model, creating a hybrid architecture.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first high-resolution, mechanistic-informed map of SIC stocks for a target watershed, directly contributing to national carbon accounting and land management. It will advance the field of digital soil assessment by demonstrating a scalable method to integrate pedogenic theory into landscape-scale prediction, strengthening the scientific basis for soil security decisions.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the explicit coupling of a process-based model of SIC dynamics with a state-of-the-art DSM workflow, moving beyond simple statistical correlation. This addresses a core challenge in upscaling soil security science by embedding mechanistic understanding of soil condition into a digital, capacity-building mapping tool.

proposals.title: Inverting Pedogenic Models: A Digital Soil Mapping Approach to Estimate Historical Microbial Respiration from Secondary Carbonate Patterns
proposals.motivation: Soil security requires understanding the historical drivers of soil condition, including the role of soil biota in carbon cycling and mineral weathering. Microbial respiration is a key process influencing carbonate precipitation, but its historical rates are difficult to quantify. This creates a critical knowledge gap for managing soil health and predicting its response to global change.
proposals.hypothesis: That spatial patterns of pedogenic carbonate (e.g., depth, morphology, abundance) recorded in soil profiles can be inverted using a process-based model to produce quantitative, spatially explicit estimates of historical microbial respiration rates.
proposals.method: We will develop an inverse modeling framework coupling a 1D reactive transport model for carbonate precipitation (e.g., based on the PHREEQC geochemical engine) with a Bayesian inversion algorithm. The approach will be applied within a Digital Soil Mapping (DSM) context, using spatially referenced soil profile data (e.g., from global databases like SoilGrids) describing carbonate distribution. The inversion will use Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) sampling to estimate posterior probability distributions for microbial respiration rate parameters, conditioned on the observed carbonate data and prior knowledge.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel methodology to reconstruct a key biological soil function from widely available geomorphic and geochemical archives. It will contribute to the 'Peak Soil' and soil security discourse by offering a quantitative tool to assess the historical capacity of soils to sustain microbial life and sequester carbon, informing management strategies for soil condition.
proposals.novelty: While forward models of carbonate formation exist, inverting them to solve for biological rate parameters is a non-trivial, ill-posed problem rarely attempted in pedology. The integration of this inverse approach within a DSM framework to produce spatial estimates of historical microbial activity represents a significant methodological advance.

proposals.title: A Digital Twin Framework for Optimizing Engineered Soil Carbon Sequestration via Nutrient Management
proposals.motivation: Soil security is critical for global food and climate security. Engineered soil carbon sequestration (SCS) through practices like enhanced weathering or biochar amendment offers a scalable negative emissions technology, but its efficiency is limited by suboptimal soil biological and chemical conditions. A digital, model-driven approach is needed to dynamically manage these conditions to maximize carbon capture and permanence, moving beyond static, one-size-fits-all application strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: That a process-based digital twin of a soil system, calibrated with proximal sensor data, can be used to design and iteratively optimize site-specific nutrient injection regimes that maximize microbial-mediated carbon stabilization while minimizing losses.
proposals.method: We will develop a mechanistic soil digital twin integrating the 3D Soil Architecture Model (3DSAM) with the Microbial-ENzyme Decomposition (MEND) model to simulate carbon dynamics. The twin will be calibrated using time-series data from in-situ sensors (e.g., CO2, moisture, temperature) and mobile phone-based aggregate stability measurements. A Bayesian optimization algorithm will be deployed on the calibrated twin to identify optimal spatio-temporal schedules for liquid nutrient injections (e.g., N, P) that maximize predicted long-term carbon storage, subject to economic and environmental constraints.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, adaptive management framework for engineered SCS, translating digital soil mapping into dynamic soil management (DSM2). It will deliver a validated, open-source protocol for optimizing carbon capture interventions, directly contributing to the operational goals of soil security. The project will also advance the role of digital twins in soil science beyond monitoring into predictive control.
proposals.novelty: While digital twins exist for industrial processes, their application to optimize *in-situ* biological soil processes for carbon capture is nascent. The integration of a mechanistic biogeochemical model with a Bayesian optimization loop for real-world nutrient management represents a significant, non-obvious advance over current empirical or static modeling approaches in soil carbon research.

ideas.title: Mechanistic Framework for Predicting Nonlinear Soil Regeneration Thresholds and Pathways
ideas.description: This research moves beyond general advocacy for nonlinear recovery by developing a falsifiable, mechanistic framework to predict it. It combines long-term field monitoring of recovering agroecosystems with controlled mesocosm experiments to identify the precise combinations of microbial inoculants, organic inputs, and plant communities that shift soil systems past degradation thresholds. The core output is a set of validated, context-sensitive models that define the conditions under which nonlinear regeneration is probable, providing a rigorous scientific basis for targeted restoration strategies.

ideas.title: Inverse Problem: Defining the Critical Transition Conditions for Peak Soil Recovery
ideas.description: This research seed expands via analogy to ecosystem ecology's regime shift theory. The original idea—defining conditions to reverse peak soil decline—parallels the inverse problem in systems like lakes or coral reefs: identifying the precise interventions needed to trigger recovery from a degraded state, not just halt decline. The analogous solution from ecology involves modeling hysteresis and defining the multi-variable thresholds (tipping points) for a critical transition back to health. Adapted to soil science, the research would frame soil recovery as a non-linear, state-transition process. It would seek to identify the combined and potentially sequential management (e.g., minimum perennial cover duration, organic amendment thresholds) and policy (e.g., payment for ecosystem service levels that change farmer risk calculus) conditions that constitute a 'recovery threshold,' providing a targeted blueprint for reversing systemic soil degradation.

ideas.title: Participatory Geostatistical Mapping for Community-Driven Soil Health
ideas.description: This research fuses geostatistical kriging with participatory design to develop a novel soil health app framework. Farmers and land managers are engaged as co-designers in workshops to define which soil parameters are mapped, how uncertainty is visualized, and what spatial scales are most useful for decision-making. Their local knowledge and on-ground observations are formally integrated as priors or validation data within the kriging process, improving model accuracy in data-sparse regions. The outcome is a community-calibrated spatial interpolation tool that produces maps which are both statistically robust and deeply aligned with the practical needs and experiential wisdom of the end-users.

ideas.title: A Global Soil Health Biopsy: Standardized, High-Resolution Degradation Tracking via Medical Imaging-Inspired Vision
ideas.description: This research fuses medical imaging techniques with standardization goals to create a 'soil health biopsy' protocol. It adapts algorithms for segmenting and tracking cells in time-lapse microscopy to automatically quantify the dispersion, stability, and morphological evolution of soil aggregates in standardized lab or field image assays. These algorithm-derived metrics—such as aggregate dispersal rates, size distribution shifts, and pore network changes—become the core, universally comparable physical indicators for a new global soil degradation framework. Consequently, the framework gains objective, high-resolution data on fundamental degradation processes (like slaking and crusting), enabling consistent, early-stage diagnosis of soil physical degradation anywhere in the world.

ideas.description: This approach overcomes the domain gap by using a physics-based simulation engine to generate vast, annotated datasets of soil aggregate dispersion under controlled virtual conditions that mimic real-world optics and material properties. A core deep learning model is first pre-trained on this synthetic data to learn robust features for segmentation and tracking in 3D. The system is then domain-adapted and validated using a strategically collected, smaller set of multi-view experimental imagery, ensuring practical accuracy. This hybrid simulation-to-real pipeline creates a generalizable tool for quantifying soil dynamics without the prohibitive cost of manually labeling thousands of real-world samples.

ideas.title: Inverse Field Diagnostics: Defining Recovery Pathways via Uncontrolled Aggregate Stability Networks
ideas.description: This research inverts the traditional soil health assessment paradigm by deploying a scalable, field-based network for continuous aggregate stability monitoring across working landscapes under entirely uncontrolled conditions. The resulting high-resolution, spatiotemporal dataset is then used as the input for an inverse modeling problem: to identify the precise sequences and combinations of management practices (e.g., cover cropping, grazing regimes, amendments) and policy interventions (e.g., subsidy timing, enforcement thresholds) that are empirically linked to measured recovery trajectories. The approach bypasses the limitations of controlled experiments to directly diagnose the causes of recovery from real-world complexity, generating evidence-based, context-specific roadmaps for achieving peak soil recovery.

ideas.title: A network-based Soil Resilience Boundary Framework for defining cross-scale operational thresholds
ideas.description: This research develops a framework to define actionable soil health thresholds for farms and landscapes by explicitly modeling them as nodes within biophysical and socio-economic networks. It combines soil process modeling with agent-based simulations to identify how local management decisions interact with cross-boundary flows of water, nutrients, and economic pressures to create system-wide tipping points. The output is a set of context-dependent, dynamic operational boundaries that account for teleconnections and feedback loops, moving beyond static, isolated farm-scale definitions of 'peak soil.'

ideas.description: This research fuses participatory design (HCI) with the challenge to linear soil degradation narratives to develop a new generation of soil health technology. It engages farmers, indigenous knowledge holders, and ecologists in co-design workshops to model and visualize nonlinear, context-specific soil recovery pathways, challenging assumptions of inevitable decline. The resulting app framework prioritizes the discovery and sharing of regenerative practices that trigger positive tipping points, rather than merely tracking degradation. This transforms the app from a diagnostic tool into a participatory platform for collectively building and validating narratives of ecological regeneration.

ideas.title: Multi-modal soil aggregate stability protocol: Validating a sensor-fusion standard beyond 2D imaging
ideas.description: This research fuses the critical examination of 2D imaging's limitations with the goal of large-scale, multi-country validation. Instead of validating the app's current image-based method as universally sufficient, the study is designed to systematically identify soil types and conditions where 2D image analysis fails to capture key breakdown dynamics (e.g., internal porosity changes, specific fracture modes). For these identified cases, the validation protocol actively incorporates and benchmarks supplementary, low-cost sensor data (such as 3D structure-from-motion, vibration, or simple mechanical probes) to fill the information gap. The outcome is the establishment of a tiered, adaptive standard method: a core 2D image protocol for suitable soils, augmented by defined multi-modal protocols for soils where imaging alone is insufficient, ensuring reliable and scientifically defensible adoption across truly diverse global pedological contexts.

ideas.title: A Context-Specific Probabilistic and Reputation-Based Validation Pipeline for Crowdsourced Soil Data
ideas.description: This research designs a novel data quality system specifically for the challenges of crowdsourced soil data, moving beyond the direct application of ecological frameworks. It combines probabilistic geospatial models to establish expected value ranges and flag anomalous submissions with a dynamic contributor reputation system that weights data based on validation against trusted local ground-truth sources. The system also mandates and verifies participant competency through interactive, in-app training on standardized sampling procedures, thereby addressing the fundamental heterogeneity and skill-dependent nature of soil data collection at its source.

ideas.title: Field-Deployable Standardized Stress Protocol (FD-SSP) for Comparable Aggregate Stability Assessment
ideas.description: This method enables field-based aggregate stability assessment by applying a controlled, standardized disturbance to soil in its natural setting. A portable tool delivers a precise sequence of wetting and kinetic energy to a prepared soil surface, simulating a defined erosive or slaking stress. The soil's response (e.g., particle detachment quantified by image analysis or turbidity of runoff) is measured to generate a stability index. This approach isolates the soil property from transient field conditions, producing data comparable across different locations, seasons, and operators while maintaining the ecological relevance of testing in-situ structure.

ideas.title: Adapting Particle Image Velocimetry from Fluid Dynamics to Track Soil Aggregate Dispersion
ideas.description: This idea expands the original seed by drawing an analogy from Experimental Fluid Dynamics. The analogous problem is tracking complex fluid motion, which is solved using Particle Image Velocimetry (PIV)—a computer vision technique that calculates velocity fields by cross-correlating the positions of tracer particles across image sequences. The adapted research idea applies this solution to soil science by treating dispersing soil aggregates as the analogous particles. By implementing PIV algorithms on video footage of soil erosion experiments, we can generate quantitative, high-resolution vector fields that map the dispersion dynamics of aggregates, offering a novel method to visualize and measure the fundamental processes of soil detachment and transport.

ideas.title: Quantifying the insufficiency of 2D image analysis for soil aggregate stability via direct 3D microstructure benchmarking
ideas.description: This research directly tests the assumption that 2D image analysis is sufficient by developing a controlled experiment where identical soil aggregates from diverse types (e.g., clay-rich, sandy, organic) undergo standardized wetting. Each aggregate is analyzed pre- and post-breakdown using both high-resolution 2D surface imaging and 3D X-ray micro-computed tomography (μCT). The system then fuses these datasets to quantify critical breakdown features—such as internal pore connectivity, crack propagation paths, and volumetric fragment distribution—that are inaccessible in 2D. By establishing statistically significant discrepancies between 2D and 3D-derived stability indices, the research provides an empirical foundation for when 3D analysis is non-negotiable, moving beyond qualitative critique to actionable, quantitative guidance.

ideas.title: A Dynamic, Continuously Validated Global Soil App Network with Embedded Adaptation
ideas.description: This initiative moves beyond single validation studies to create a permanent, operational infrastructure for ongoing performance monitoring and algorithmic adaptation. It deploys a globally distributed network of instrumented validation sites that continuously feed ground-truth data, which is combined with anonymized, quality-checked user data to create a real-time accuracy dashboard. This system triggers scheduled, region-specific model updates and allows for the rapid integration of new soil-crop-climate modules, ensuring the app remains a reliable, evolving standard rather than a static tool validated for a past condition.

ideas.title: Adapting Financial Stress Testing for Soil Resilience Assessment
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution to a parallel problem in financial economics—assessing systemic resilience through stress testing—to the context of soil condition assessment. In finance, stress tests model the impact of severe scenarios on institutions to find failure thresholds and recovery capacity. The adapted research idea applies this framework to soil systems by developing a protocol to model soil responses to defined stress scenarios (e.g., drought, compaction). By simulating impacts on key state variables, the method aims to identify soil system thresholds, quantify pre-threshold buffering capacity, and assess regenerative potential, providing a structured, scenario-based analytical method grounded in resilience theory for soil health diagnosis and management.

ideas.title: A Multi-Platform Sensor Network for Ubiquitous Soil Measurement, Inspired by Oceanographic Observing Systems
ideas.description: This research seed challenges the assumption that the mobile phone is the only viable ubiquitous platform for soil measurement by drawing an analogy from oceanography. Oceanographers solved the analogous problem of limited single-platform data (e.g., from ships) by creating integrated observing systems that combine fixed buoys, seafloor sensors, autonomous vehicles, and satellites. Adapting this solution, the research will investigate a tiered, multi-platform network for soil: fixed smart sensors for long-term temporal data, drones for on-demand spatial mapping, and satellite imagery for large-area context. The core investigation will be designing the data architecture and cost-benefit framework for this heterogeneous system, determining how to optimally blend these platforms to achieve ubiquitous, scalable soil measurement beyond the smartphone.

ideas.title: Apply Community-Based Participatory Research from Public Health to Soil App Design
ideas.description: This idea adapts the Community-Based Participatory Research (CBPR) method from Public Health to the development of a soil health app. In CBPR, researchers and community members are equal partners throughout the entire research and intervention design process to ensure cultural relevance and adoption. By formally applying this framework, the project would establish a long-term co-design partnership with a specific farming community (e.g., a regional cooperative). The farmers would be engaged as equal partners in an iterative cycle to define the app's core problems, prototype features like data entry and visualization, and field-test the tool's utility on their own operations. This moves beyond standard participatory design workshops to create a soil health app that is deeply actionable, contextually appropriate, and trusted by its end-users.

ideas.title: Adapting Microfluidic Droplet Analysis for Phone-Based Micro-Aggregate Stability
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from microfluidics and soft matter physics, where the stability of microscopic droplets is assessed using camera-based imaging under stress. The analogous method uses high-speed imaging through microscopes to track droplet deformation and critical breakup points. We adapt this by scaling down the phone-based macro-aggregate analysis method. A smartphone is fitted with a clip-on macro/micro lens to image soil micro-aggregates suspended in a shallow, transparent flow cell. By controlling fluid flow rate to apply precise shear stress, the phone records video of the aggregates. Adapted image analysis software, based on microfluidic droplet tracking algorithms, then quantifies micro-aggregate stability by measuring the flow conditions or shear stress at which they begin to disperse or break apart.

ideas.title: Adapting Seismic Tomography for 3D Soil Aggregate Characterization
ideas.description: This idea challenges the core assumption that 2D image analysis captures all relevant aggregate breakdown dynamics for every soil type by drawing an analogy from geophysics. In seismology, 2D surface maps are insufficient for subsurface modeling, leading to the development of seismic tomography, which uses multi-angle wave propagation data to reconstruct 3D internal structures. Adapting this solution, the proposed research would develop a soil analysis method where controlled acoustic/ultrasonic pulses are transmitted through a soil sample from various angles. The measured wave properties (velocity, attenuation) would be fused with surface image data in a computational inversion model to reconstruct the critical 3D internal architecture and mechanical connectivity of aggregates, providing a dynamic physical understanding that pure 2D visual analysis misses.

ideas.title: Adapting Seismic Tomography's Velocity Field Interpolation for App-Based Spatial Data
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from geophysical seismic tomography, where sparse, indirect measurements are inverted to create continuous 3D models of subsurface properties. The analogous solution uses an inverse modeling framework with spatial covariance constraints to interpolate between discrete data points. Adapted to the original context, we propose applying this inverse problem framework to point-based app measurements. The region is discretized into a grid, and the app data, potentially unevenly sampled, are treated as observations. By defining a forward model based on spatial interpolation (e.g., using a variogram model) and solving the inverse problem (e.g., via Bayesian inversion or least-squares with regularization), we can generate a robust, continuous map that optimally fits the sparse measurements while respecting the spatial structure learned from geostatistics, offering a potentially more flexible and statistically rigorous alternative to direct kriging for digital soil mapping or similar applications.

ideas.title: Metaphors are sufficient for scientific assessment

ideas.title: Field-Based Soil Aggregate Stability via Passive Acoustic Sensing
ideas.description: This idea inverts the need for a controlled laboratory setting by adapting a solution from Structural Health Monitoring in Civil Engineering. In that field, passive acoustic emission sensors are used to assess material integrity (like bridge concrete) under real-world, uncontrolled loads by detecting and classifying stress-induced acoustic signatures. The adapted research idea involves deploying field-hardened acoustic sensors within soil to passively record the sounds of aggregate breakdown during natural environmental cycles (rain, drought, frost). By applying machine learning pattern recognition to these acoustic emissions, the method would correlate specific sound signatures—such as pops, cracks, or shifts—with different stages and severities of aggregate disintegration, enabling continuous, quantitative stability assessment directly in uncontrolled field conditions.

ideas.title: Quantifying and Balancing Expert Efficiency and Novice Learnability in Scientific App Interfaces
ideas.description: This research develops a framework to systematically evaluate and design interfaces for scientific mobile apps by explicitly measuring the tension between expert user productivity and novice user accessibility. Instead of iterating based on aggregated feedback, the system employs controlled experiments comparing interface variants against objective metrics: task completion time and error rate for domain experts, versus time-to-competence and success rate for first-time users from the non-scientific community. The outcome is a set of evidence-based design principles and, where possible, adaptive interface mechanisms that optimize for both cohorts without compromising the application's core scientific utility.

ideas.title: Deconfounded Spatial Field Estimation for Behavioral Sensor Networks
ideas.description: This research develops a framework to separate behavioral confounding from genuine spatial signal in crowdsourced point data before interpolation. A hierarchical model first estimates a latent spatial field jointly with parameters for non-spatial confounders like user density and time-of-day effects. Geostatistical kriging is then applied rigorously to the posterior distribution of this purified spatial field, not the raw measurements. The output is a continuous map with associated uncertainty that accurately represents the spatial structure of the target phenomenon, robust to the sampling biases inherent in opportunistic mobile sensing.

ideas.title: Structured Knowledge Sovereignty Protocols for Equitable Method Co-Creation
ideas.description: This research designs and tests a formal protocol for method co-creation that structurally prevents the capture or dilution of non-expert knowledge. It begins by facilitating separate, in-context knowledge articulation sessions with specific end-user cohorts to document their needs, metrics of success, and tacit practices without scientific framing. These distinct knowledge systems are then brought into a structured negotiation and integration phase with scientists, using transparent deliberative mapping to track the provenance and integration of all design inputs. The resulting co-designed method is validated not just by outcome, but by the integrity of the knowledge-integration process itself, ensuring the method is robustly grounded in equitable, practical expertise.

ideas.title: Adaptive Interface Refinement via Co-Design with Diverse User Personas
ideas.description: This research idea adapts the participatory co-design methodology from urban planning to the problem of refining a scientific app's user-friendliness for diverse communities. Instead of merely collecting separate feedback streams, the project would implement structured, facilitated co-design workshops where representative users from scientific and non-scientific backgrounds simultaneously engage with interactive app prototypes. This analogous solution forces the direct confrontation of jargon, workflow, and interface complexity conflicts in real-time, guiding an iterative design process toward universally intuitive interfaces that are scientifically rigorous yet accessible, much like a public space designed for multiple concurrent user groups.

ideas.title: Adversarial Deliberation Frameworks for Policy Assessment
ideas.description: This approach develops structured assessment systems that force the explicit translation between metaphors/narratives and quantitative indicators through adversarial processes. Competing stakeholder groups must defend their preferred policy metaphors by linking them to specific, measurable multidimensional outcomes, while opposing groups attempt to falsify these links or propose superior metaphorical-quantitative pairings. The framework outputs not a single score but a contested map of evidence linking qualitative understandings to quantitative data, making value conflicts and measurement uncertainties central to the assessment. This moves beyond merely demanding quantification to rigorously testing how different conceptualizations of a problem hold up against evidence and competing interpretations.

ideas.title: Institutional Frameworks as 'Clinical Pathways' for Technology Adoption
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from Evidence-Based Medicine, this study analyzes how institutions facilitate tech adoption by adapting the solution of 'clinical pathways.' In healthcare, institutions standardize the adoption of new practices via multidisciplinary care protocols that translate evidence into localized workflows. Similarly, this research will investigate how public and private institutions can create and implement structured 'technology adoption pathways' for scientific tools. These pathways would act as institutional frameworks that codify implementation steps, define stakeholder roles, mandate necessary training, and align incentives to systematically bridge the gap between cutting-edge tool development and their broad, reliable utilization by wider scientific and technical communities.

ideas.title: Mechanisms of Equitable Adoption: A Comparative Study of Institutional Configurations for Overcoming Specific Tech Implementation Barriers
ideas.description: This research moves beyond describing institutional roles to explicitly testing how different institutional configurations causally impact equitable adoption outcomes. It begins by identifying concrete, high-priority barriers to equitable access (e.g., proprietary data lock-in, infrastructure cost distribution). The core methodology then involves selecting matched pairs or sets of implementation cases facing similar barriers but involving different institutional setups (e.g., public-private partnership vs. community-led coalition). Through in-depth comparative process-tracing, the study isolates the specific operational mechanisms within successful institutions that directly overcome the barrier, generating actionable design principles for building more effective adoption pathways.
